text: The surgeon encounters adhesions of the omentum to the top of the gallbladder, which is tensely distended and difficult to grasp. The gallbladder is surrounded by adherent pericolic fat and more omentum, and the surgeon carefully dissects these tissues to properly identify the gallbladder.
The surgeon carefully peels down the omentum, revealing the underlying duodenum. The duodenum is drawn up and needs to be taken down to expose the bottom of the gallbladder.

The surgeon begins working to clear the peritoneum around the gallbladder, which is chronically thickened and inflamed. Lateral mobilization of the gallbladder is performed to aid in retraction and exposure, with the lateral peritoneum being opened.

The surgeon inadvertently creates a small hole in the gallbladder, revealing it is filled with pus. The surgeon continues to work to locate the bottom of the gallbladder and its junction with the cystic duct, navigating through the unforgiving tissues.

The surgeon identifies the borders of the Calot's triangle and attempts to clear the posterior area where the cystic duct is anticipated, facing challenges due to the angles and contact with the gallbladder.

The surgeon identifies the artery and carefully frees it, aiming to reduce bleeding and create more space for dissection. The peritoneum on the inferior surface of the duct is examined and removed, providing direct access to the duct.
The surgeon clips and transects the cystic duct after performing a cholangiogram through a hole in the gallbladder, confirming the anatomy. A posterior cystic artery is visible behind the duct.
The surgeon detaches the gallbladder from the liver bed, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field. The area appears dry, indicating successful control of bleeding.
